refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 requires quantification of the availability target and clarification of measurement scope. Link availability is a system-level quality attribute that depends on network infrastructure, redundancy mechanisms, and fault detection/recovery. The refined FR explicitly defines the metric (availability ratio), threshold (≥0.999), and measurement period to enable objective verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculation of cumulative downtime; comparison against 0.999 threshold. Requires instrumentation of link health status and timestamped event logging.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures within [X] seconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate failover or graceful degradation upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log link state events with timestamps for availability calculation.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant communication paths or recovery mechanisms to support ≥0.999 availability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual-path network architecture (primary + backup link) with automatic failover and health monitoring at the Teleoperation module.
DP-B: Implement network redundancy and link health monitoring distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules with coordinated failover logic.
DP-C: Implement centralized link availability management service within Safety Services that monitors and enforces availability SLAs across all communication paths.
DP-D: Implement link availability through a combination of network-level redundancy (dual carriers/paths) and application-level health checks with automatic recovery.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement link availability through a combination of network-level redundancy (dual carriers/paths) and application-level health checks with automatic recovery.

potential_sources: DP-D couples network infrastructure decisions (carrier selection, path redundancy) with application-level monitoring logic (health checks, recovery triggers). This is acceptable because both layers are necessary to achieve 0.999 availability; however, the coupling must be managed via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) to avoid tight architectural binding.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D avoids over-specification of a single module (e.g., DP-A would over-constrain Teleoperation) and avoids under-specification (DP-C would create a new service boundary). The dual-layer approach is justified by the high safety criticality and the need for both infrastructure resilience and application-level observability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure can provide dual-path capability (e.g., dual LTE carriers, satellite backup, or wired + wireless). Risk: If customer site has single-carrier constraint, DP-D may not be feasible; mitigation requires early site survey.

Assumption: Application-level health checks can detect link degradation faster than network-layer timeouts. Risk: Misconfiguration of health check thresholds could trigger false failovers; mitigation requires tuning and validation.

Assumption: Failover latency is acceptable to safety and operational requirements. Risk: If failover latency exceeds operator tolerance, availability target may be unachievable; mitigation requires latency specification in sub-FR-010.2.
Risk: Dual-path redundancy increases cost and complexity; customer may not accept trade-off. Mitigation: Validate cost-benefit against availability target early.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) network-layer redundancy strategy (e.g., dual LTE + satellite, or dual fiber), (2) application-level health check interval and failure detection threshold, (3) failover latency target, and (4) fallback behavior if both paths fail (e.g., safe stop, manual takeover). This will reduce ambiguity during IM assignment.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the application-level link health monitoring, failover logic, and command/telemetry routing. It interfaces with the Vehicle module for operational state and with Safety Services for safety-critical alerts. Network-layer redundancy (dual carriers/paths) is external to this module and managed by network infrastructure; however, Teleoperation must be designed to exploit dual-path capability via the Network Link interface.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives vehicle state, sensor data, and link health status from Vehicle module. Used to assess operational readiness and detect anomalies.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends operator commands and control signals to Vehicle module. Teleoperation module must ensure Tasking is only sent when link is healthy; otherwise, queues or rejects commands.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Sends link failure alerts and availability status to Safety Services. Enables safety-critical decision-making (e.g., emergency stop, operator notification).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel(s) to remote operator. Teleoperation module monitors link health, detects failures, and switches between primary and backup paths if available.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link health monitoring and failover logic. High containment because the module's primary responsibility is managing the remote operator connection. Low diffusion because health checks and failover are localized to this module. Minor diffusion risk: if Vehicle module also implements link health checks (redundant monitoring), this creates diffusion; mitigation is to centralize all health checks in Teleoperation.

Vehicle module could implement link health checks, but this would diffuse availability logic across two modules. Vehicle's primary responsibility is vehicle control and sensor management, not network availability. Assigning IM to Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle state and link state, complicating testing and maintenance.

Safety Services could own link availability monitoring as a safety-critical service, but this would over-extend its scope. Safety Services should react to link failures (via SafetyAlert) rather than own the detection logic. Assigning IM to Safety Services would create a new dependency on network infrastructure that is outside Safety Services' traditional domain.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container and should not own implementation details. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse availability logic across all sub-modules and violate separation of concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it owns the remote operator interface and is responsible for command/telemetry routing, (2) link health monitoring is a core function of teleoperation systems, (3) failover logic is localized to this module and does not require coordination with Vehicle or Safety Services (beyond alerting), and (4) this assignment minimizes diffusion and maintains clear architectural boundaries.
containment_risks: Risk: If network infrastructure does not provide dual-path capability, Teleoperation cannot implement failover logic, and DP-D becomes infeasible. Mitigation: Validate network infrastructure early; if single-path only, revise DP to focus on application-level recovery (e.g., reconnection logic, buffering).

Risk: Teleoperation module may become overloaded if it also owns command queuing, operator UI, and vehicle state caching. Mitigation: Ensure Teleoperation is focused on link management; delegate other responsibilities to separate modules or sub-components.

Risk: If Vehicle module also implements link health checks (e.g., for autonomous fallback), this creates diffusion and potential inconsistency. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership: Teleoperation owns link health monitoring; Vehicle owns autonomous fallback logic (separate concern).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between Teleoperation's link health monitoring and Vehicle's autonomous fallback behavior. Specifically: (1) Teleoperation detects link failures and alerts Safety Services via SafetyAlert, (2) Vehicle receives SafetyAlert and executes pre-programmed safe stop or autonomous behavior, (3) Teleoperation does not directly control Vehicle's fallback logic. This separation ensures that link availability (DP-D) is owned by Teleoperation, while safety response is owned by Vehicle/Safety Services.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (dual-path network redundancy + application-level health checks) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP is specific enough to guide IM assignment and feasible given typical network infrastructure. The coupling between network-layer and application-layer is acceptable and justified by the high safety criticality. No revision needed at this gate.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If Vehicle module also implements link health checks for autonomous fallback, this creates redundant monitoring logic. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership (Teleoperation owns link health; Vehicle owns fallback behavior) and document the interface (SafetyAlert) that connects them.

Diffusion Risk 2: If Safety Services also monitors link availability for safety compliance, this creates a second monitoring path. Mitigation: Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert from Teleoperation rather than independently monitoring the link.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the optimal IM boundary with a containment score of 0.95. The module owns the Network Link interface and is responsible for command/telemetry routing, making it the natural owner of link health monitoring and failover logic. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not require new interface definitions. No revision needed at this gate.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-010 is verifiable via continuous monitoring of link state during the evaluation period. Teleoperation module must log link state transitions (up/down) with timestamps, enabling calculation of availability ratio. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of Network Link interface to detect failures, (2) timestamped event logging, (3) post-evaluation analysis of logs. This is feasible and does not require new verification methods.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module with clear ownership. Sub-FRs (010.1, 010.2, 010.3, 010.4) are allocated as follows: 010.1 (failure detection) → Teleoperation, 010.2 (failover) → Teleoperation, 010.3 (logging) → Teleoperation, 010.4 (network infrastructure) → external (not owned by NGHE Solution). This allocation is clear and does not create ambiguity. No revision needed at this gate.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Refine DP-D to specify network-layer redundancy strategy (e.g., dual LTE carriers, satellite backup) and application-level health check parameters (interval, threshold, failover latency). This will reduce ambiguity during detailed design.

Revision 2: Document the interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle for SafetyAlert. Specifically, define the alert types (e.g., LINK_FAILURE, LINK_DEGRADED) and expected Vehicle responses (e.g., safe stop, autonomous behavior).

Revision 3: Establish a test plan for link availability verification. Define the evaluation period, measurement methodology, and acceptance criteria. Ensure that the test plan accounts for both network-layer failures (e.g., carrier outage) and application-layer failures (e.g., health check timeout).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs to guide detailed design. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (010.1 through 010.4) that address distinct aspects of link availability: failure detection, failover, logging, and network infrastructure. Each sub-FR requires its own FR-DP-IM decomposition to ensure complete coverage and clear ownership. DP-D and Teleoperation IM are sound and pass all gates, but further decomposition is necessary to achieve design completeness.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures within [X] seconds of occurrence.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate failover to backup path or graceful degradation upon link loss, with failover latency ≤ [Y] seconds.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log link state events (up/down transitions) with timestamps for post-evaluation availability calculation.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant communication paths (e.g., dual carriers, satellite backup) to support ≥0.999 availability.

assumptions: Assumption 1: Network infrastructure can provide dual-path capability (e.g., dual LTE carriers, satellite backup, or wired + wireless). This is a critical assumption that must be validated during site survey.

Assumption 2: Application-level health checks can detect link degradation faster than network-layer timeouts. This requires tuning of health check parameters (interval, threshold) based on network characteristics.
Assumption 3: Failover latency is acceptable to safety and operational requirements. This must be specified in sub-FR-010.2 and validated against operator tolerance.

Assumption 4: Teleoperation module can be designed to exploit dual-path capability without requiring changes to Vehicle or Safety Services modules. This assumes clean interface boundaries (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link).
Assumption 5: Customer-defined availability target is ≥0.999 (99.9%). If customer specifies a different target (e.g., 0.99 or 0.9999), DP-D and IM assignment may need revision.
open_risks: Risk 1: Network infrastructure may not support dual-path redundancy due to site constraints (e.g., single carrier coverage, no satellite access). Mitigation: Conduct early site survey and validate network options. If dual-path is not feasible, revise DP-D to focus on application-level recovery (e.g., reconnection logic, buffering).

Risk 2: Failover latency may exceed operator tolerance, making 0.999 availability unachievable. Mitigation: Specify failover latency target in sub-FR-010.2 and validate against operator requirements early.

Risk 3: Dual-path redundancy increases cost and complexity. Customer may not accept trade-off. Mitigation: Conduct cost-benefit analysis and present options (e.g., single-path with application-level recovery vs. dual-path with automatic failover).

Risk 4: Teleoperation module may become overloaded if it also owns command queuing, operator UI, and vehicle state caching. Mitigation: Ensure Teleoperation is focused on link management; delegate other responsibilities to separate modules.

Risk 5: Vehicle module may also implement link health checks for autonomous fallback, creating diffusion and potential inconsistency. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership and document interface contracts (SafetyAlert).
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Site survey report confirming network infrastructure options (dual carriers, satellite, wired backup, etc.).
Evidence 2: Network latency and reliability data for candidate carriers/paths to support DP-D feasibility analysis.
Evidence 3: Operator requirements for failover latency and acceptable downtime (to validate sub-FR-010.2).
Evidence 4: Cost-benefit analysis comparing single-path vs. dual-path redundancy options.
Evidence 5: Detailed design of Teleoperation module's link health monitoring and failover logic (to support sub-FR-010.1 and 010.2).
Evidence 6: Test plan for link availability verification, including evaluation period, measurement methodology, and acceptance criteria.
Evidence 7: Interface specification for SafetyAlert (to clarify boundary between Teleoperation and Vehicle/Safety Services).
